title: Italy Pivots Toward Delhi: How Rome's New Government Rewires Europe's India Strategy
lede: Modi's bilateral with Meloni in Hiroshima signals a structural realignment in India-Italy ties, moving beyond personality-driven diplomacy toward institutionalized partnership anchored in shared positions on global governance.

sections.context:
The Hiroshima bilateral represents the first high-level reset of India-Italy relations under Giorgia Meloni's government, and its timing is strategically deliberate. Meloni assumed office in September 2022 as head of a center-right coalition, and her government has already demonstrated a willingness to decouple from some of the EU's rigid positions on India—particularly on questions of democratic credentials and human rights governance that had become friction points under previous Italian administrations. The Modi-Meloni meeting occurred during India's participation in the G7 as a guest, itself a symbolic affirmation of Delhi's rising centrality to Western-led governance forums. By scheduling a productive bilateral rather than treating it as a perfunctory handshake, both sides signaled intent to move beyond the irritants that had accumulated during earlier diplomatic cycles.

The agreement to develop a "structured partnership framework" is the operative phrase here. This is not rhetorical flourish; it represents a conscious shift from ad-hoc engagement to institutionalized mechanisms. Previous India-Italy relations operated through periodic high-level visits and sector-specific dialogues, but lacked the systematic architecture that characterizes India's partnerships with France, Germany, or Japan. Meloni's government appears willing to invest in this architecture, partly because her coalition's ideological positioning—national sovereignty, strategic autonomy, skepticism of multilateral constraints—aligns more naturally with India's stated foreign policy objectives than did the previous government's approach.
sections.threads:
This bilateral must be read alongside [[india-france-strategic-partnership-2022|India's deepening strategic partnership with France]], which has become the template for how Europe can engage India without the friction of lecturing on internal governance. Macron's government demonstrated that respecting Indian autonomy on policy choices while building defense and technological cooperation yields dividends in influence and alignment on global issues. Meloni appears to be adopting a similar playbook, having signaled interest in defense cooperation and technology partnerships that require fewer ideological preconditions than EU-wide frameworks typically demand. The timing also intersects with [[european-union-india-trade-talks-2023|ongoing EU-India trade negotiations]], where Rome's positioning within European councils will matter; a warmer bilateral relationship gives Italy incentive to argue for more pragmatic terms that benefit Indian interests without compromising EU coherence.

Historically, India-Italy relations have been episodic rather than strategic—strong personalities (like Modi and former PM Matteo Renzi) would generate momentum that would dissipate when governments changed or attention shifted. The [[india-italy-bilateral-2018|2018-2020 period saw notable irritants around defense procurement, UNESCO votes, and governance criticism]] that cooled the relationship considerably. Meloni's government represents a potential break from this pattern because her coalition is less invested in the EU consensus mechanisms that previously constrained Italian bilateral autonomy. Additionally, Italy's historical role as the G7 sherpa for India matters; as a sitting G7 member willing to champion Indian interests in forums like the upcoming G20 presidency, Italy can amplify India's voice in ways that require less friction with its European partners.

The structured framework also reflects a broader European recognition that engagement with India requires different diplomatic architecture than engagement with China or Russia. Unlike those powers, India is neither a peer competitor to the West nor a revisionist state; it is an autonomous middle power with whom alignment is possible on specific issues (maritime security, technology standards, climate finance) without requiring ideological conformity. Meloni's government has internalized this distinction more quickly than some of its European counterparts.
sections.signal:
Watch for the announcement and activation of specific institutional mechanisms within the next nine to twelve months. The structured partnership framework will mean little if it remains a negotiating text; the critical indicator will be whether India-Italy establish joint working groups on defense cooperation, technology standards, or trade facilitation that actually meet on a scheduled calendar. Look specifically for: (1) any defense equipment or technology co-development announcements, (2) a high-level dialogue mechanism at the foreign secretary or deputy foreign minister level that meets semi-annually, and (3) Italian positioning within EU forums (trade talks, critical minerals discussions, standards-setting bodies) that favors Indian interests or at minimum abstains from coordinated positions that India opposes.

Secondly, monitor whether Italy's warming toward India creates visible friction within EU consensus-building on India-related issues. The EU tends to operate through consensus, which means individual member states rarely break ranks visibly. If Italy begins either blocking or abstaining on India-critical votes in EU bodies—particularly on matters like GSP+ benefits or critical minerals supply chains—that would signal that the Modi-Meloni relationship has created genuine policy divergence rather than mere bilateral cordiality. This would also indicate whether Meloni's government is willing to pay costs (within European peer pressure) to advance the relationship.

Thirdly, track the degree to which this bilateral relationship expands beyond Modi-Meloni personal rapport to embed itself in institutional memory and lower-level bureaucratic relationships. The presence of structured mechanisms matters less than whether Indian and Italian officials at the working level actually internalize the priority of the relationship and defend it during multilateral negotiations. This is the difference between a reset that lasts and one that evaporates when one of the principals leaves office.
